topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the kitchen counter like a declaration of war. Liam stared at the address, his breath catching in short, sharp bursts. Across the city. Three thousand miles. He picked up his phone, his thumb hovering over his friend’s contact, a tremor running through his hand. "No," he muttered, the sound rough in his throat. He slammed the phone down, the plastic rattling against the granite. He paced the length of the living room three times, his jaw tight enough to crack enamel. Every familiar corner of his apartment suddenly felt alien, tainted by the impending distance. He kicked at a loose floorboard, the sudden, violent motion betraying the churning storm inside him. He needed to talk to him, to demand an explanation, but the words felt trapped behind a wall of sheer, boiling frustration.

[story 2]

"You're serious?" Maya’s voice was dangerously level, stripped bare of its usual warmth. She gripped the letter so hard the paper crinkled beneath her knuckles. The sheer audacity of it made her stomach clench with a hot, sickening feeling. She stood by the window, watching the familiar streetlights blur into streaks of yellow. Her knuckles were white where she clenched her fists at her sides. A low growl started deep in her chest, vibrating up into her throat. She could feel the blood pounding behind her eyes, a frantic, insistent rhythm. She didn't want to cry; she just wanted to shout until the glass in the window shattered. The thought of him leaving, of this shared history dissolving into mere geography, felt like a physical blow.

[story 3]

I read the text message again, the screen illuminating my face in a harsh, unforgiving light. The words—'Moving to Seattle next month'—seemed to mock me. My hands started shaking uncontrollably; I had to press them flat against the desk to stop the tremors. A deep, guttural sound escaped my chest, something raw and ugly. I pushed my chair back so fast it scraped loudly against the hardwood floor. I walked to the door, my movements jerky and sharp, and slammed it shut with such force the frame shuddered. I leaned against the wood, sucking in ragged, shallow breaths, trying desperately to force the tidal wave of fury down, but it only surged, making my vision swim.
